CAMAS COMMONS
SAN JUAN ISLAND, WASHINGTON

CAMAS COMMONS - A Regenerative Farming Land Trust

Making farming more affordable.

Welcome to a new model for farming. We are Ally and Guisepi, two land stewards & community enthusiasts who desire to see farming continue on our little island in the PNW. We’re in a critical moment of losing young, capable producers due to lack of affordability of land so we’re creating Camas Commons to answer the call.

— Ally & Guisepi

What is Camas Commons?

Camas Commons is a nonprofit regenerative farming land trust being created to provide perpetually affordable residential and agricultural leases to farmers and producers on San Juan Island, WA. Our goals lie in regenerating the land, supporting the local community with local goods and events, creating land accessibility for young farmers, and preserving healthy land and housing for future generations.

We are partnering with On Sacred Ground Land Trust, a local 501(c)3 non-profit, and other organizations to make this happen.

How can I support this project?

We have been unexpectedly entered into a competitive proposal process due in September for acquiring the land we currently live on. If chosen, we get to stay farming the land we love, in the house we’ve made a home in the place where we first fell in love and later got married. Our primary need right now is Angel Donors - people willing to leave a legacy by donating funds necessary to purchasing this land, thus making it perpetually affordable and in conservation for farmers, producers, and land stewards.

Our first major fundraising deadline is mid September, when we make our final proposal to the Land Bank. Yes, it’s steep, $1.5 million! Can we do it? Let’s find out…will you help us? Every. Little. Bit. Counts.

Why Supporting Our Land Trust Matters…

We believe this land is meant to be shared, and with us stewarding it, you can guarantee this legacy we’ve begun will continue. This project aims to help alleviate many of the issues facing our community, like young people leaving the island, farmers aging-out, skyrocketing land and housing costs, supply chain issues, and more. Creating this land trust will help maintain a web of resilience in our local community though food sovereignty, land connection, and attracting and keeping talented makers and growers on our island.

FAQ:

What is this land all about?

This 120-acre farm has seen a lot — a historic dairy, an eccentric follower of the spiritual leader Ramtha, a golf course, and an organic farm. For the past 20 years, this farm has been known as Talking Horse Ranch, after the famed “talking horse” purchased by previous owner Ernie Kanzler Jr. from the channeler of his spiritual guru, Ramtha.

For these past 20-years the farm has been under-managed, and kept running at a low-idle. Most of the infrastructure is in disrepair, but not beyond the point-of-no-return.

Despite all this, the land is a wonderfully magical place — with excellent topography, landscape, a seasonal creek, 6 ponds, enchanting forests, and incredibly cool and quirky buildings and landscaping. Most of the developed land is south facing slope with excellent sun, the wells produce an incredible 45 and 70 GPM, and the big barn offers 13,000 square feet of usable indoor space. These are just a few of the significant attributes.

40 of the 120 acres will be purchased for Camas Commons, with the other 80 acres entering into perpetual conservation through the San Juan Conservation Land Bank. Together, public access will be included in this plan with a walking trail that connects the Beaverton Marsh Preserve to the top of Beaver Hill, through Camas Commons.

How do the leases work?

Residential Members will have the option to own their own home and sign a 99-year lease, or lease a home from the land trust. They, and Non-Residential Members, will have the opportunity to lease land, infrastructure, or buildings with short-term leases for agricultural, traditional craft, forestry, or other sustainability-related endeavors.

How many people will live at Camas Commons?

Based on the allowed density, four houses plus the existing ADU are permissible. Averaging 2-3 people per house, we see 10-15 full time residents. At least one member from each house must be involved in agriculture, sustainability-adjacent activities, or an administrative role.

What will this project offer the local community?

Members of Camas Commons will be producing agricultural products, like fruits, vegetables, eggs and more, as well as useful and traditional crafts and land-connection events. There may also be educational workshops, seasonal gatherings, petting zoos, live music, a farm store and more.

How can I get a lease at Camas Commons?

Applications for residential leases will open within a year of the purchase of the land. Agricultural leases may open sooner than that. If you want to be on a list, please contact us at info@camascommons.org

Who is spearheading Camas Commons?

Current Talking Horse Ranch land stewards Guisepi Spadafora and Ally Rugge have been helping facilitate a path towards this goal for the past several years. Guisepi grew up on the island, and after years of service-based travel (i.e. the Free Tea Bus [freeteaparty.org]), returned to the islands with his now wife, Ally, to find the right piece of land to dedicate themselves to. He has 20 years of experience in the nonprofit world, and loves to tinker. Ally has lived and worked on Talking Horse Ranch three times in the past 15 years, learning to grow food, rehabilitate soil, and raise chickens and dairy goats ([women-who-stare-at-goats-collective]). They got married on the farm in 2023.

How can I support Camas Commons?

Please consider pledging or donating money to our cause. All pledges will only be collected if our proposal is chosen and we make our fundraising goals.

Secondarily, please let us know if you or someone you know wants to volunteer and has skills in:

  • Nonprofit management
  • Land use and permitting
  • Fundraising
  • Grant writing
  • Permaculture design

CAMAS COMMONS — A Regenerative Farming Land Trust

San Juan Island, Washington • info@camascommons.org
